scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced the category workflow through readiness, active execution, and completion only when item-master, inventory, release, and publication evidence were explicit.
Missing approved GTIN, released inventory, and lot evidence kept the work blocked and produced a bounded exception note rather than an unsupported activation recommendation.
Assortment strategy and pricing approval authority were rejected cleanly and routed to the correct commercial owners.
Conflicting lot, inventory, and activation evidence across ERP, WMS, and publication records was treated as a traceability blocker and prevented unsupported completion.
A quality hold moved the workflow into hold posture, preserved evidence, and kept final release authority with QA.
Completion was refused until publication, sync, and release-dependent evidence existed, keeping lifecycle state transitions reversible when evidence failed.
Low-confidence handling correctly requested hold rules, approved-system ownership, and escalation maps rather than guessing.
Conflicting public-baseline and tenant-policy signals were summarized and escalated instead of being merged into a false approval answer.
The lane rejected regulatory and label-compliance approval ownership and routed the request to the correct reviewers.
Supplier substitution pressure was escalated to supplier-quality, procurement, and QA change-control owners without unsupported approval.
Tenant terminology was adapted without losing FDA and GS1 baseline controls, lifecycle-state discipline, or explicit assumptions.

notes: All 11 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to CPG category execution, item-master readiness, customer-impact control, traceability, and completion evidence.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the audit contract.
